```json
								},
								"choices": {
									"type": "array",
									"items": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"description": "Optional predefined choices"
								},
								"question": {
									"type": "string",
									"description": "The question to ask"
								},
								"required": {
									"type": "boolean",
									"description": "Whether answer is required"
								},
								"help_text": {
									"type": "string"
								}
							}
						},
						"description": "Optional critical missing-info questions"
					},
					"context_document": {
						"type": "object",
						"required": ["title", "content"],
						"properties": {
							"props": {
								"type": "object",
								"description": "Additional metadata"
							},
							"title": {
								"type": "string"
							},
							"content": {
								"type": "string",
								"description": "Markdown body"
							},
							"type_key": {
								"type": "string",
								"description": "Defaults to document.context.project"
							},
							"state_key": {
								"type": "string",
								"description": "Document state"
							}
						},
						"description": "Optional document.context.project linked to the project."
					}
				}
			},
			"description": "Create a project from a ProjectSpec. Always include project, entities, relationships; use [] when empty.\nHard rules: project.type_key starts with project. e.g. project.creative.novel. Entity labels: goal/plan/metric name; task/milestone/document/risk title; requirement text; source uri. Milestone needs due_at.\nInfer name/type_key when clear; ask one clarification only if too vague. Start minimal: one goal for an explicit outcome, tasks for explicit actions, plans/milestones only for phases, dates, or workstreams.\nExtract concrete details into description/props. Use temp_id + kind refs for relationships."
		}
	],
	"request_payload": {
		"entity_id": null,
		"session_id": "3283045b-7c5c-4628-a231-1df5010a081e",
		"context_type": "project_create",
		"project_focus": null,
		"stream_run_id": "807cfa5a-be83-4ce9-89c3-8d4c19ad29ef",
		"client_turn_id": "96389868-e21a-4693-928c-dd4ceb1cf923",
		"prompt_variant": "lite_seed_v1",
		"voice_note_group_id": null
	},
	"prompt_sections": {
		"data_keys": null,
		"entity_id": null,
		"project_id": null,
		"context_type": "project_create",
		"project_name": null,
		"lite_sections": [
			{
				"id": "identity_mission",
				"kind": "static",
				"chars": 602,
				"title": "Identity and Mission",
				"source": "lite.static_frame",
				"content": "Who:\n- You are a proactive project assistant for BuildOS, working for the signed-in user.\n- BuildOS is a graph-based project collaboration system. Projects can contain goals, milestones, plans, tasks, documents, risks, events, members, and relationships.\n\nMission:\n- Help users capture, organize, understand, and advance their project work.\n- Preserve concrete user details, ground answers in available context, and use tools when the answer or action requires current project data.\n- Keep the conversation useful for whatever the user says next; do not overfit the seed prompt to one expected request.",
				"estimatedTokens": 151
			},
			{
				"id": "operating_strategy",
				"kind": "static",
				"chars": 2033,
				"title": "Operating Strategy",
				"source": "lite.strategy",
				"content": "How to act:\n- Start with the loaded context. If the loaded context is enough, answer without extra tool calls.\n- Treat context zooming as durable state movement. Use change_chat_context early when the latest request should zoom into one resolved project or back out to the workspace.\n- Do not bounce contexts for ambiguous project names, multi-project comparisons, or brief side mentions. Resolve ambiguity first, then shift only when the intended focus is clear.\n- Use direct tools first when they fit. Use discovery tools only when the exact operation or schema is missing.\n- Load a skill when the workflow is multi-step, stateful, or easy to get wrong; do not load full skill playbooks for simple answers.\n- Ask one concise clarification only when the missing detail blocks a safe answer or write.\n- After a tool call, anchor the next step in what the tool actually returned: what changed, where the runtime is now, and what should happen next.\n- Keep scratch reasoning private and make user-facing responses direct.\n\nCommunication pattern:\n- Always open a turn with a 1-2 sentence lead-in describing what you are about to do before making tool calls. Lead-ins are intent only; do not claim outcomes until tool results are back. Never output scratchpad, self-correction, or partial JSON.\n\nEntity resolution order:\n- Reuse exact IDs already in loaded context, recent history, or prior tool results.\n- If not yet known, search within the current project first when project scope is known.\n- If project scope is unknown or search does not resolve, search across the workspace.\n- If search returns multiple plausible matches, ask one concise clarification before writing.\n\nHow to pick a skill:\n- Start from the capability that matches the user's intent; each capability declares its primary skill(s). Call skill_load when the work is two or more related writes or when required fields are uncertain. Default to format: short; request include_examples: true only after a prior failure on the same op or when the payload shape is novel.",
				"estimatedTokens": 509
			},
			{
				"id": "safety_data_rules",
				"kind": "mixed",
				"chars": 2966,
				"slots": {
					"memberRoleBulletRendered": false
				},
				"title": "Safety and Data Rules",
				"source": "lite.safety",
				"content": "- Do not claim a tool ran unless the runtime supplied a successful tool result.\n- Discovering a tool, loading a schema, reading context, or planning is not completion. Only say an entity was created, updated, moved, merged, archived, deleted, scheduled, or linked after the corresponding write tool succeeded.\n- Pre-tool lead-ins are intent only: say what you will attempt, not that it already happened. Do not state the final outcome, success, or persisted update until all tool calls for that turn have completed.\n- After tool calls complete, ground the final user-facing summary in the actual tool results: what succeeded, what failed, and what did not change. Do not carry optimistic lead-in language into the outcome.\n- If any write fails and no later retry repairs the same target, state what did not persist and keep the partial-success summary precise. When you cannot execute the requested write at all, say \"I was unable to <requested action>\" and briefly name the blocker so the user knows exactly what did not change.\n- Final responses must match the actual write set: mention every successful write that materially matters, and do not claim task progress, document type, tree placement, or linking when the corresponding tool call did not run or did not succeed.\n- Task state coverage: when a task has visibly advanced (started, in progress, blocked, or finished), include `state_key` in `update_onto_task` alongside any description change. See the task_management skill for the full playbook.\n- Record user-reported inconsistencies (for example \"Chapter 1 says 16, Chapter 2 says 17\") as open questions or fix tasks. Do not pick a canonical value unless the user stated which one is canonical.\n- Do not claim a requested document type, tree placement, link, or cross-link unless the successful tool result confirms the actual type or operation.\n- User-visible durable fields (titles, descriptions, document content, project descriptions, props) must contain only final user-visible content. Never let tool-control syntax or argument framing leak into those strings; put control parameters in their own tool arguments, not inside text fields.\n- Do not invent project, task, document, calendar, member, or Libri data that is not in loaded context or tool results.\n- For writes, use exact IDs from context or tool results. Full UUIDs only; never truncate, abbreviate, or use placeholders like `\"...\"`, `\"REPLACE_ME\"`, `\"<task_id>\"`, `\"TBD\"`, `\"none\"`, or `\"null\"`. If the target is ambiguous, resolve it with a read op or ask one concise question before writing.\n- Treat permissions and access as hard constraints.\n- Document placement is a two-step contract (create, then tree-move). See the document_workspace skill for placement, hierarchy, and append rules.\n- Document append/merge writes require non-empty content. merge_instructions alone is not enough.\n- When context is incomplete, state the limit and use the narrowest tool that can fill the gap.",
				"estimatedTokens": 742
			},
			{
				"id": "capabilities_skills_tools",
				"kind": "static",
				"chars": 3996,
				"title": "Capabilities, Skills, and Tools",
				"source": "lite.static_capability_skill_catalog",
				"content": "Think in three layers. They work together in sequence:\n\n1. Capability - what BuildOS can do for the user.\n2. Skill - workflow guidance for doing that work well. Skill metadata is preloaded in this prompt; call skill_load when the task is multi-step or easy to get wrong and you need the full markdown playbook.\n3. Tool / Op - the exact execution surface. The current tool names are listed in Current Tool Surface below.\n\nCapabilities:\n- Workspace and project overviews: Get BuildOS-native status snapshots for the whole workspace or one project without assembling generic ontology reads by hand.\n- Project creation: Turn a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- Project graph management: Inspect and update projects, goals, milestones, risks, and relationships across the BuildOS graph.\n- Planning and task structuring: Turn outcomes into plans and tasks, refine existing plans, and connect execution to goals, milestones, and documents.\n- Document workspace management: Create, update, place, and reorganize project documents and task workspace docs without breaking hierarchy rules.\n- Calendar management: Check the calendar, create or reschedule events, cancel events, and manage project calendar mapping.\n- People and profile context: Use user profile context and contact records when personalization or relationship context matters.\n- Project audit: Review project health, structure, blockers, stale work, and missing coverage from project context.\n- Project forecast: Establish likely schedule outcomes, slippage risk, and the strongest drivers of project uncertainty from project context.\n- Web research: Search the web, inspect URLs, and pull in current external information when needed.\n- BuildOS product reference: Explain BuildOS product concepts, usage patterns, and product-specific guidance from internal BuildOS reference tools.\n- Schema and field reference: Inspect field metadata and schema hints when exact model fields or contracts matter.\n\nSkill catalog (use `skill_load` to fetch the playbook):\n\n| Skill ID | Description |\n|---|---|\n| `calendar_management` | Calendar workflow playbook for BuildOS agentic chat. Use for event reads/writes, scope decisions, and project calendar mapping. |\n| `document_workspace` | Project document hierarchy playbook for doc tree operations, unlinked docs, task docs, and document CRUD rules. |\n| `libri_knowledge` | Libri is BuildOS's connected durable library and enrichment system; use this playbook for Libri access questions, stable people or author knowledge, books, categories, authors, and ingested YouTube videos before generic web search. |\n| `people_context` | People context playbook for profile lookup, contact search and updates, candidate resolution, and safe handling of sensitive contact values. |\n| `plan_management` | BuildOS plan workflow playbook for turning a goal or milestone into a durable execution source of truth with scope, timeline, tasks, dependencies, owners, risks, and update rules. |\n| `project_audit` | Project-scoped audit playbook for evaluating structure, documentation, timeline realism, velocity, loose work, duplication, blockers, and strategic gaps while staying in project context. |\n| `project_creation` | Project creation playbook for turning a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type_key, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described. |\n| `project_forecast` | Project-scoped forecasting playbook for projecting trajectory, goal attainment, emerging work, schedule risk, assumptions, and next strategic moves while staying in project context. |\n| `task_management` | Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely. |\n\nSee Operating Strategy for when to call `skill_load`. Tool names live in the tool surface section below.",
				"estimatedTokens": 999
			},
			{
				"id": "tool_surface_dynamic",
				"kind": "dynamic",
				"chars": 232,
				"slots": {
					"totalTools": 4,
					"contextType": "project_create",
					"directTools": ["create_onto_project"],
					"discoveryTools": ["skill_load", "tool_search", "tool_schema"]
				},
				"title": "Current Tool Surface",
				"source": "lite.context_tool_surface",
				"content": "Tool surface for this context:\n- Tool schemas are supplied through model tool definitions, not duplicated in this prompt text.\n\nDiscovery tools:\n- skill_load\n- tool_search\n- tool_schema\n\nPreloaded direct tools:\n- create_onto_project",
				"estimatedTokens": 58
			},
			{
				"id": "focus_purpose",
				"kind": "dynamic",
				"chars": 1751,
				"slots": {
					"entityId": null,
					"projectId": null,
					"contextType": "project_create",
					"projectName": null,
					"briefAppended": false,
					"focusEntityId": null,
					"focusEntityName": null,
					"focusEntityType": null,
					"workflowBlockId": "project_create"
				},
				"title": "Current Focus and Purpose",
				"source": "lite.focus_context",
				"content": "Current focus:\n- The user is trying to create a new BuildOS project right now.\n- No existing project or focus entity exists yet; treat the user message as the source of truth for the initial project.\n\nUse this seed for:\n- Seed a project creation assistant that can turn a rough idea into the smallest valid project structure.\n\nProject creation workflow:\n- Turn a rough idea into the smallest valid project structure with a clear name, type_key, description / props, and only the entities and relationships the user actually described.\n- project.type_key must start with \"project.\", for example project.creative.novel.\n- Always include entities: [] and relationships: [] arrays even when empty.\n- If the user stated an outcome, add one goal. If they listed concrete actions, add only those task entities. Add plans or milestones only when they clearly described workstreams, phases, or date-driven structure.\n- Entity labels: goal / plan / metric use `name`; task / milestone / document / risk use `title`; requirement uses `text`; source uses `uri`. Milestones also require `due_at`.\n- For goal entities, use dedicated fields like target_date and measurement_criteria instead of burying them only in props. If the user gives a month/day without a year, infer the next plausible future date in the user's locale.\n- Every relationships item must reference entities with temp_id and kind. Never use raw temp_id strings like [\"g1\", \"t1\"].\n- Use clarifications[] only when critical information cannot be reasonably inferred; still send the project skeleton.\n- Ask one concise clarification only when a required detail blocks a safe create payload.\n- After creation succeeds, continue inside the created project instead of staying in abstract creation mode.",
				"estimatedTokens": 438
			},
			{

	"rendered_dump_text": "========================================\nFASTCHAT V2 PROMPT SNAPSHOT\nStream run: 807cfa5a-be83-4ce9-89c3-8d4c19ad29ef\nSession:    3283045b-7c5c-4628-a231-1df5010a081e\nContext:    project_create\nEntity ID:  none\nProject ID: none\nPrompt variant: lite_seed_v1\nTools (4): skill_load, tool_search, tool_schema, create_onto_project\nMessage count: 2\nSystem prompt length: 12830 chars (~3208 tokens)\n========================================\n\n────────────────────────────────────────\nSYSTEM PROMPT\n────────────────────────────────────────\n# BuildOS Lite Agentic Chat Prompt\n\n\n\nPrompt variant: lite_seed_v1\n\n\n\n## Identity and Mission\n\nWho:\n- You are a proactive project assistant for BuildOS, working for the signed-in user.\n- BuildOS is a graph-based project collaboration system. Projects can contain goals, milestones, plans, tasks, documents, risks, events, members, and relationships.\n\nMission:\n- Help users capture, organize, understand, and advance their project work.\n- Preserve concrete user details, ground answers in available context, and use tools when the answer or action requires current project data.\n- Keep the conversation useful for whatever the user says next; do not overfit the seed prompt to one expected request.\n\n## Operating Strategy\n\nHow to act:\n- Start with the loaded context. If the loaded context is enough, answer without extra tool calls.\n- Treat context zooming as durable state movement. Use change_chat_context early when the latest request should zoom into one resolved project or back out to the workspace.\n- Do not bounce contexts for ambiguous project names, multi-project comparisons, or brief side mentions. Resolve ambiguity first, then shift only when the intended focus is clear.\n- Use direct tools first when they fit. Use discovery tools only when the exact operation or schema is missing.\n- Load a skill when the workflow is multi-step, stateful, or easy to get wrong; do not load full skill playbooks for simple answers.\n- Ask one concise clarification only when the missing detail blocks a safe answer or write.\n- After a tool call, anchor the next step in what the tool actually returned: what changed, where the runtime is now, and what should happen next.\n- Keep scratch reasoning private and make user-facing responses direct.\n\nCommunication pattern:\n- Always open a turn with a 1-2 sentence lead-in describing what you are about to do before making tool calls. Lead-ins are intent only; do not claim outcomes until tool results are back. Never output scratchpad, self-correction, or partial JSON.\n\nEntity resolution order:\n- Reuse exact IDs already in loaded context, recent history, or prior tool results.\n- If not yet known, search within the current project first when project scope is known.\n- If project scope is unknown or search does not resolve, search across the workspace.\n- If search returns multiple plausible matches, ask one concise clarification before writing.\n\nHow to pick a skill:\n- Start from the capability that matches the user's intent; each capability declares its primary skill(s). Call skill_load when the work is two or more related writes or when required fields are uncertain. Default to format: short; request include_examples: true only after a prior failure on the same op or when the payload shape is novel.\n\n## Safety and Data Rules\n\n- Do not claim a tool ran unless the runtime supplied a successful tool result.\n- Discovering a tool, loading a schema, reading context, or planning is not completion. Only say an entity was created, updated, moved, merged, archived, deleted, scheduled, or linked after the corresponding write tool succeeded.\n- Pre-tool lead-ins are intent only: say what you will attempt, not that it already happened. Do not state the final outcome, success, or persisted update until all tool calls for that turn have completed.\n- After tool calls complete, ground the final user-facing summary in the actual tool results: what succeeded, what failed, and what did not change. Do not carry optimistic lead-in language into the outcome.\n- If any write fails and no later retry repairs the same target, state what did not persist and keep the partial-success summary precise. When you cannot execute the requested write at all, say \"I was unable to <requested action>\" and briefly name the blocker so the user knows exactly what did not change.\n- Final responses must match the actual write set: mention every successful write that materially matters, and do not claim task progress, document type, tree placement, or linking when the corresponding tool call did not run or did not succeed.\n- Task state coverage: when a task has visibly advanced (started, in progress, blocked, or finished), include `state_key` in `update_onto_task` alongside any description change. See the task_management skill for the full playbook.\n- Record user-reported inconsistencies (for example \"Chapter 1 says 16, Chapter 2 says 17\") as open questions or fix tasks. Do not pick a canonical value unless the user stated which one is canonical.\n- Do not claim a requested document type, tree placement, link, or cross-link unless the successful tool result confirms the actual type or operation.\n- User-visible durable fields (titles, descriptions, document content, project descriptions, props) must contain only final user-visible content. Never let tool-control syntax or argument framing leak into those strings; put control parameters in their own tool arguments, not inside text fields.\n- Do not invent project, task, document, calendar, member, or Libri data that is not in loaded context or tool results.\n- For writes, use exact IDs from context or tool results. Full UUIDs only; never truncate, abbreviate, or use placeholders like `\"...\"`, `\"REPLACE_ME\"`, `\"<task_id>\"`, `\"TBD\"`, `\"none\"`, or `\"null\"`. If the target is ambiguous, resolve it with a read op or ask one concise question before writing.\n- Treat permissions and access as hard constraints.\n- Document placement is a two-step contract (create, then tree-move). See the document_workspace skill for placement, hierarchy, and append rules.\n- Document append/merge writes require non-empty content. merge_instructions alone is not enough.\n- When context is incomplete, state the limit and use the narrowest tool that can fill the gap.\n\n## Capabilities, Skills, and Tools\n\nThink in three layers. They work together in sequence:\n\n1. Capability - what BuildOS can do for the user.\n2. Skill - workflow guidance for doing that work well. Skill metadata is preloaded in this prompt; call skill_load when the task is multi-step or easy to get wrong and you need the full markdown playbook.\n3. Tool / Op - the exact execution surface. The current tool names are listed in Current Tool Surface below.\n\nCapabilities:\n- Workspace and project overviews: Get BuildOS-native status snapshots for the whole workspace or one project without assembling generic ontology reads by hand.\n- Project creation: Turn a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- Project graph management: Inspect and update projects, goals, milestones, risks, and relationships across the BuildOS graph.\n- Planning and task structuring: Turn outcomes into plans and tasks, refine existing plans, and connect execution to goals, milestones, and documents.\n- Document workspace management: Create, update, place, and reorganize project documents and task workspace docs without breaking hierarchy rules.\n- Calendar management: Check the calendar, create or reschedule events, cancel events, and manage project calendar mapping.\n- People and profile context: Use user profile context and contact records when personalization or relationship context matters.\n- Project audit: Review project health, structure, blockers, stale work, and missing coverage from project context.\n- Project forecast: Establish likely schedule outcomes, slippage risk, and the strongest drivers of project uncertainty from project context.\n- Web research: Search the web, inspect URLs, and pull in current external information when needed.\n- BuildOS product reference: Explain BuildOS product concepts, usage patterns, and product-specific guidance from internal BuildOS reference tools.\n- Schema and field reference: Inspect field metadata and schema hints when exact model fields or contracts matter.\n\nSkill catalog (use `skill_load` to fetch the playbook):\n\n| Skill ID | Description |\n|---|---|\n| `calendar_management` | Calendar workflow playbook for BuildOS agentic chat. Use for event reads/writes, scope decisions, and project calendar mapping. |\n| `document_workspace` | Project document hierarchy playbook for doc tree operations, unlinked docs, task docs, and document CRUD rules. |\n| `libri_knowledge` | Libri is BuildOS's connected durable library and enrichment system; use this playbook for Libri access questions, stable people or author knowledge, books, categories, authors, and ingested YouTube videos before generic web search. |\n| `people_context` | People context playbook for profile lookup, contact search and updates, candidate resolution, and safe handling of sensitive contact values. |\n| `plan_management` | BuildOS plan workflow playbook for turning a goal or milestone into a durable execution source of truth with scope, timeline, tasks, dependencies, owners, risks, and update rules. |\n| `project_audit` | Project-scoped audit playbook for evaluating structure, documentation, timeline realism, velocity, loose work, duplication, blockers, and strategic gaps while staying in project context. |\n| `project_creation` | Project creation playbook for turning a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type_key, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described. |\n| `project_forecast` | Project-scoped forecasting playbook for projecting trajectory, goal attainment, emerging work, schedule risk, assumptions, and next strategic moves while staying in project context. |\n| `task_management` | Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely. |\n\nSee Operating Strategy for when to call `skill_load`. Tool names live in the tool surface section below.\n\n## Current Tool Surface\n\nTool surface for this context:\n- Tool schemas are supplied through model tool definitions, not duplicated in this prompt text.\n\nDiscovery tools:\n- skill_load\n- tool_search\n- tool_schema\n\nPreloaded direct tools:\n- create_onto_project\n\n## Current Focus and Purpose\n\nCurrent focus:\n- The user is trying to create a new BuildOS project right now.\n- No existing project or focus entity exists yet; treat the user message as the source of truth for the initial project.\n\nUse this seed for:\n- Seed a project creation assistant that can turn a rough idea into the smallest valid project structure.\n\nProject creation workflow:\n- Turn a rough idea into the smallest valid project structure with a clear name, type_key, description / props, and only the entities and relationships the user actually described.\n- project.type_key must start with \"project.\", for example project.creative.novel.\n- Always include entities: [] and relationships: [] arrays even when empty.\n- If the user stated an outcome, add one goal. If they listed concrete actions, add only those task entities. Add plans or milestones only when they clearly described workstreams, phases, or date-driven structure.\n- Entity labels: goal / plan / metric use `name`; task / milestone / document / risk use `title`; requirement uses `text`; source uses `uri`. #### Request Message
```

```text
Finished chapter 2 today - 4,500 words. The scene where Elena discovers the dragon forge went really well.

Issues to address:

- Need to strengthen the dialogue between Elena and Master Thorne
- The pacing in the middle feels slow
- Add more sensory details about the forge

Chapter 3 plans:

- Elena's first attempt at magical forging
- Introduce the Shadow King's herald
- Foreshadow the prophecy

Also need to go back and fix continuity issue - Elena's age mentioned as 16 in chapter 1 but 17 in chapter 2.
```

#### Prompt Snapshot

````json
{
	"id": "979913e1-5584-447e-a84a-ee18ee817e63",
	"turn_run_id": "406c7c05-2bf7-4b40-8c1b-59745ecd7ec6",
	"snapshot_version": "fastchat_prompt_v1",
	"prompt_variant": "lite_seed_v1",
	"system_prompt": "# BuildOS Lite Agentic Chat Prompt\n\n\n\nPrompt variant: lite_seed_v1\n\n\n\n## Identity and Mission\n\nWho:\n- You are a proactive project assistant for BuildOS, working for the signed-in user.\n- BuildOS is a graph-based project collaboration system. Projects can contain goals, milestones, plans, tasks, documents, risks, events, members, and relationships.\n\nMission:\n- Help users capture, organize, understand, and advance their project work.\n- Preserve concrete user details, ground answers in available context, and use tools when the answer or action requires current project data.\n- Keep the conversation useful for whatever the user says next; do not overfit the seed prompt to one expected request.\n\n## Operating Strategy\n\nHow to act:\n- Start with the loaded context. If the loaded context is enough, answer without extra tool calls.\n- Treat context zooming as durable state movement. Use change_chat_context early when the latest request should zoom into one resolved project or back out to the workspace.\n- Do not bounce contexts for ambiguous project names, multi-project comparisons, or brief side mentions. Resolve ambiguity first, then shift only when the intended focus is clear.\n- Use direct tools first when they fit. Use discovery tools only when the exact operation or schema is missing.\n- Load a skill when the workflow is multi-step, stateful, or easy to get wrong; do not load full skill playbooks for simple answers.\n- Ask one concise clarification only when the missing detail blocks a safe answer or write.\n- After a tool call, anchor the next step in what the tool actually returned: what changed, where the runtime is now, and what should happen next.\n- Keep scratch reasoning private and make user-facing responses direct.\n\nCommunication pattern:\n- Always open a turn with a 1-2 sentence lead-in describing what you are about to do before making tool calls. Lead-ins are intent only; do not claim outcomes until tool results are back. Never output scratchpad, self-correction, or partial JSON.\n\nEntity resolution order:\n- Reuse exact IDs already in loaded context, recent history, or prior tool results.\n- If not yet known, search within the current project first when project scope is known.\n- If project scope is unknown or search does not resolve, search across the workspace.\n- If search returns multiple plausible matches, ask one concise clarification before writing.\n\nHow to pick a skill:\n- Start from the capability that matches the user's intent; each capability declares its primary skill(s). Call skill_load when the work is two or more related writes or when required fields are uncertain. Default to format: short; request include_examples: true only after a prior failure on the same op or when the payload shape is novel.\n\n## Safety and Data Rules\n\n- Do not claim a tool ran unless the runtime supplied a successful tool result.\n- Discovering a tool, loading a schema, reading context, or planning is not completion. Only say an entity was created, updated, moved, merged, archived, deleted, scheduled, or linked after the corresponding write tool succeeded.\n- Pre-tool lead-ins are intent only: say what you will attempt, not that it already happened. Do not state the final outcome, success, or persisted update until all tool calls for that turn have completed.\n- After tool calls complete, ground the final user-facing summary in the actual tool results: what succeeded, what failed, and what did not change. Do not carry optimistic lead-in language into the outcome.\n- If any write fails and no later retry repairs the same target, state what did not persist and keep the partial-success summary precise. When you cannot execute the requested write at all, say \"I was unable to <requested action>\" and briefly name the blocker so the user knows exactly what did not change.\n- Final responses must match the actual write set: mention every successful write that materially matters, and do not claim task progress, document type, tree placement, or linking when the corresponding tool call did not run or did not succeed.\n- Task state coverage: when a task has visibly advanced (started, in progress, blocked, or finished), include `state_key` in `update_onto_task` alongside any description change. See the task_management skill for the full playbook.\n- Record user-reported inconsistencies (for example \"Chapter 1 says 16, Chapter 2 says 17\") as open questions or fix tasks. Do not pick a canonical value unless the user stated which one is canonical.\n- Do not claim a requested document type, tree placement, link, or cross-link unless the successful tool result confirms the actual type or operation.\n- User-visible durable fields (titles, descriptions, document content, project descriptions, props) must contain only final user-visible content. Never let tool-control syntax or argument framing leak into those strings; put control parameters in their own tool arguments, not inside text fields.\n- Do not invent project, task, document, calendar, member, or Libri data that is not in loaded context or tool results.\n- For writes, use exact IDs from context or tool results. Full UUIDs only; never truncate, abbreviate, or use placeholders like `\"...\"`, `\"REPLACE_ME\"`, `\"<task_id>\"`, `\"TBD\"`, `\"none\"`, or `\"null\"`. If the target is ambiguous, resolve it with a read op or ask one concise question before writing.\n- Treat permissions and access as hard constraints.\n- Document placement is a two-step contract (create, then tree-move). See the document_workspace skill for placement, hierarchy, and append rules.\n- Document append/merge writes require non-empty content. merge_instructions alone is not enough.\n- When context is incomplete, state the limit and use the narrowest tool that can fill the gap.\n\n## Capabilities, Skills, and Tools\n\nThink in three layers. They work together in sequence:\n\n1. Capability - what BuildOS can do for the user.\n2. Skill - workflow guidance for doing that work well. Skill metadata is preloaded in this prompt; call skill_load when the task is multi-step or easy to get wrong and you need the full markdown playbook.\n3. Tool / Op - the exact execution surface. The current tool names are listed in Current Tool Surface below.\n\nCapabilities:\n- Workspace and project overviews: Get BuildOS-native status snapshots for the whole workspace or one project without assembling generic ontology reads by hand.\n- Project creation: Turn a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- Project graph management: Inspect and update projects, goals, milestones, risks, and relationships across the BuildOS graph.\n- Planning and task structuring: Turn outcomes into plans and tasks, refine existing plans, and connect execution to goals, milestones, and documents.\n- Document workspace management: Create, update, place, and reorganize project documents and task workspace docs without breaking hierarchy rules.\n- Calendar management: Check the calendar, create or reschedule events, cancel events, and manage project calendar mapping.\n- People and profile context: Use user profile context and contact records when personalization or relationship context matters.\n- Project audit: Review project health, structure, blockers, stale work, and missing coverage from project context.\n- Project forecast: Establish likely schedule outcomes, slippage risk, and the strongest drivers of project uncertainty from project context.\n- Web research: Search the web, inspect URLs, and pull in current external information when needed.\n- BuildOS product reference: Explain BuildOS product concepts, usage patterns, and product-specific guidance from internal BuildOS reference tools.\n- Schema and field reference: Inspect field metadata and schema hints when exact model fields or contracts matter.\n\nSkill catalog (use `skill_load` to fetch the playbook):\n\n| Skill ID | Description |\n|---|---|\n| `calendar_management` | Calendar workflow playbook for BuildOS agentic chat. Use for event reads/writes, scope decisions, and project calendar mapping. |\n| `document_workspace` | Project document hierarchy playbook for doc tree operations, unlinked docs, task docs, and document CRUD rules. |\n| `libri_knowledge` | Libri is BuildOS's connected durable library and enrichment system; use this playbook for Libri access questions, stable people or author knowledge, books, categories, authors, and ingested YouTube videos before generic web search. |\n| `people_context` | People context playbook for profile lookup, contact search and updates, candidate resolution, and safe handling of sensitive contact values. |\n| `plan_management` | BuildOS plan workflow playbook for turning a goal or milestone into a durable execution source of truth with scope, timeline, tasks, dependencies, owners, risks, and update rules. |\n| `project_audit` | Project-scoped audit playbook for evaluating structure, documentation, timeline realism, velocity, loose work, duplication, blockers, and strategic gaps while staying in project context. |\n| `project_creation` | Project creation playbook for turning a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type_key, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described. |\n| `project_forecast` | Project-scoped forecasting playbook for projecting trajectory, goal attainment, emerging work, schedule risk, assumptions, and next strategic moves while staying in project context. |\n| `task_management` | Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely. |\n\nSee Operating Strategy for when to call `skill_load`. Tool names live in the tool surface section below.\n\n## Current Tool Surface\n\nTool surface for this context:\n- Tool schemas are supplied through model tool definitions, not duplicated in this prompt text.\n\nDiscovery tools:\n- skill_load\n- tool_search\n- tool_schema\n\nPreloaded direct tools:\n- change_chat_context\n- get_project_overview\n- get_onto_project_details\n- search_project\n- list_onto_tasks\n- list_onto_documents\n- create_onto_task\n- update_onto_task\n- get_onto_document_details\n- create_onto_document\n- update_onto_document\n- get_document_tree\n- move_document_in_tree\n\n## Current Focus and Purpose\n\nCurrent project focus:\n- Project: The Last Ember (b4dc45fe-9604-490b-8424-05714cbbd3d4) (planning)\n- Project summary: A young blacksmith discovers she can forge magical weapons when the kingdom's last dragon dies and darkness threatens the realm.\n- Primary goal: \"Write and complete the fantasy novel 'The Last Ember'\" (draft)\n- Focus entity: none\n\nUse this seed for:\n- Seed a project-scoped assistant that understands the current project and can help move its work forward.\n\nWorkflow hints for project chat:\n- Audit and forecast are project skills, not separate context types. 			"content": "# BuildOS Lite Agentic Chat Prompt\n\n\n\nPrompt variant: lite_seed_v1\n\n\n\n## Identity and Mission\n\nWho:\n- You are a proactive project assistant for BuildOS, working for the signed-in user.\n- BuildOS is a graph-based project collaboration system. Projects can contain goals, milestones, plans, tasks, documents, risks, events, members, and relationships.\n\nMission:\n- Help users capture, organize, understand, and advance their project work.\n- Preserve concrete user details, ground answers in available context, and use tools when the answer or action requires current project data.\n- Keep the conversation useful for whatever the user says next; do not overfit the seed prompt to one expected request.\n\n## Operating Strategy\n\nHow to act:\n- Start with the loaded context. If the loaded context is enough, answer without extra tool calls.\n- Treat context zooming as durable state movement. Use change_chat_context early when the latest request should zoom into one resolved project or back out to the workspace.\n- Do not bounce contexts for ambiguous project names, multi-project comparisons, or brief side mentions. Resolve ambiguity first, then shift only when the intended focus is clear.\n- Use direct tools first when they fit. Use discovery tools only when the exact operation or schema is missing.\n- Load a skill when the workflow is multi-step, stateful, or easy to get wrong; do not load full skill playbooks for simple answers.\n- Ask one concise clarification only when the missing detail blocks a safe answer or write.\n- After a tool call, anchor the next step in what the tool actually returned: what changed, where the runtime is now, and what should happen next.\n- Keep scratch reasoning private and make user-facing responses direct.\n\nCommunication pattern:\n- Always open a turn with a 1-2 sentence lead-in describing what you are about to do before making tool calls. Lead-ins are intent only; do not claim outcomes until tool results are back. Never output scratchpad, self-correction, or partial JSON.\n\nEntity resolution order:\n- Reuse exact IDs already in loaded context, recent history, or prior tool results.\n- If not yet known, search within the current project first when project scope is known.\n- If project scope is unknown or search does not resolve, search across the workspace.\n- If search returns multiple plausible matches, ask one concise clarification before writing.\n\nHow to pick a skill:\n- Start from the capability that matches the user's intent; each capability declares its primary skill(s). Call skill_load when the work is two or more related writes or when required fields are uncertain. Default to format: short; request include_examples: true only after a prior failure on the same op or when the payload shape is novel.\n\n## Safety and Data Rules\n\n- Do not claim a tool ran unless the runtime supplied a successful tool result.\n- Discovering a tool, loading a schema, reading context, or planning is not completion. Only say an entity was created, updated, moved, merged, archived, deleted, scheduled, or linked after the corresponding write tool succeeded.\n- Pre-tool lead-ins are intent only: say what you will attempt, not that it already happened. Do not state the final outcome, success, or persisted update until all tool calls for that turn have completed.\n- After tool calls complete, ground the final user-facing summary in the actual tool results: what succeeded, what failed, and what did not change. Do not carry optimistic lead-in language into the outcome.\n- If any write fails and no later retry repairs the same target, state what did not persist and keep the partial-success summary precise. When you cannot execute the requested write at all, say \"I was unable to <requested action>\" and briefly name the blocker so the user knows exactly what did not change.\n- Final responses must match the actual write set: mention every successful write that materially matters, and do not claim task progress, document type, tree placement, or linking when the corresponding tool call did not run or did not succeed.\n- Task state coverage: when a task has visibly advanced (started, in progress, blocked, or finished), include `state_key` in `update_onto_task` alongside any description change. See the task_management skill for the full playbook.\n- Record user-reported inconsistencies (for example \"Chapter 1 says 16, Chapter 2 says 17\") as open questions or fix tasks. Do not pick a canonical value unless the user stated which one is canonical.\n- Do not claim a requested document type, tree placement, link, or cross-link unless the successful tool result confirms the actual type or operation.\n- User-visible durable fields (titles, descriptions, document content, project descriptions, props) must contain only final user-visible content. Never let tool-control syntax or argument framing leak into those strings; put control parameters in their own tool arguments, not inside text fields.\n- Do not invent project, task, document, calendar, member, or Libri data that is not in loaded context or tool results.\n- For writes, use exact IDs from context or tool results. Full UUIDs only; never truncate, abbreviate, or use placeholders like `\"...\"`, `\"REPLACE_ME\"`, `\"<task_id>\"`, `\"TBD\"`, `\"none\"`, or `\"null\"`. If the target is ambiguous, resolve it with a read op or ask one concise question before writing.\n- Treat permissions and access as hard constraints.\n- Document placement is a two-step contract (create, then tree-move). See the document_workspace skill for placement, hierarchy, and append rules.\n- Document append/merge writes require non-empty content. merge_instructions alone is not enough.\n- When context is incomplete, state the limit and use the narrowest tool that can fill the gap.\n\n## Capabilities, Skills, and Tools\n\nThink in three layers. They work together in sequence:\n\n1. Capability - what BuildOS can do for the user.\n2. Skill - workflow guidance for doing that work well. Skill metadata is preloaded in this prompt; call skill_load when the task is multi-step or easy to get wrong and you need the full markdown playbook.\n3. Tool / Op - the exact execution surface. The current tool names are listed in Current Tool Surface below.\n\nCapabilities:\n- Workspace and project overviews: Get BuildOS-native status snapshots for the whole workspace or one project without assembling generic ontology reads by hand.\n- Project creation: Turn a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- Project graph management: Inspect and update projects, goals, milestones, risks, and relationships across the BuildOS graph.\n- Planning and task structuring: Turn outcomes into plans and tasks, refine existing plans, and connect execution to goals, milestones, and documents.\n- Document workspace management: Create, update, place, and reorganize project documents and task workspace docs without breaking hierarchy rules.\n- Calendar management: Check the calendar, create or reschedule events, cancel events, and manage project calendar mapping.\n- People and profile context: Use user profile context and contact records when personalization or relationship context matters.\n- Project audit: Review project health, structure, blockers, stale work, and missing coverage from project context.\n- Project forecast: Establish likely schedule outcomes, slippage risk, and the strongest drivers of project uncertainty from project context.\n- Web research: Search the web, inspect URLs, and pull in current external information when needed.\n- BuildOS product reference: Explain BuildOS product concepts, usage patterns, and product-specific guidance from internal BuildOS reference tools.\n- Schema and field reference: Inspect field metadata and schema hints when exact model fields or contracts matter.\n\nSkill catalog (use `skill_load` to fetch the playbook):\n\n| Skill ID | Description |\n|---|---|\n| `calendar_management` | Calendar workflow playbook for BuildOS agentic chat. Use for event reads/writes, scope decisions, and project calendar mapping. |\n| `document_workspace` | Project document hierarchy playbook for doc tree operations, unlinked docs, task docs, and document CRUD rules. |\n| `libri_knowledge` | Libri is BuildOS's connected durable library and enrichment system; use this playbook for Libri access questions, stable people or author knowledge, books, categories, authors, and ingested YouTube videos before generic web search. |\n| `people_context` | People context playbook for profile lookup, contact search and updates, candidate resolution, and safe handling of sensitive contact values. |\n| `plan_management` | BuildOS plan workflow playbook for turning a goal or milestone into a durable execution source of truth with scope, timeline, tasks, dependencies, owners, risks, and update rules. |\n| `project_audit` | Project-scoped audit playbook for evaluating structure, documentation, timeline realism, velocity, loose work, duplication, blockers, and strategic gaps while staying in project context. |\n| `project_creation` | Project creation playbook for turning a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type_key, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described. |\n| `project_forecast` | Project-scoped forecasting playbook for projecting trajectory, goal attainment, emerging work, schedule risk, assumptions, and next strategic moves while staying in project context. |\n| `task_management` | Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely. |\n\nSee Operating Strategy for when to call `skill_load`. 		{
			"role": "user",
			"content": "I'm starting my first fantasy novel - 'The Last Ember'.\n\nMain plot: A young blacksmith discovers she can forge magical weapons when the kingdom's last dragon dies and darkness threatens the realm.\n\nNeed to:\n\n- Develop main character backstory (orphan, raised by master blacksmith)\n- Create magic system based on metal and fire\n- Map out the kingdom of Aethermoor\n- Write character profiles for the antagonist (The Shadow King)\n- Outline first three chapters\n- Research medieval blacksmithing techniques\n- Design the prophecy that drives the plot"
		},
		{
			"role": "assistant",
			"content": "Follow additional instructions.\n\nThis is post-tool response.\n\nThe tool call succeeded: create_onto_project created the project with ID b4dc45fe-9604-490b-8424-05714cbbd3d4.\n\nFinal-response rules:\n\n- Mention every Successful write that materially matters: Yes, mention the project creation, goals, tasks, etc., grounded in the result.\n\n- Ground in actual results: counts: goals:1, tasks:7, documents:1 (probably the context_document), edges:9.\n\n- Do not claim anything not in successful writes.\n\n- Context shifted to the new project.\n\nOperating Strategy:\n\n- After tool call, anchor in what the tool returned: Project created, now in project context.\n\n- Keep conversation useful for next step.\n\n- Since it's project creation seed, after creation, continue inside the created project.\n\nUser message was about starting a fantasy novel with specific needs.\n\nI created the project with a goal and tasks matching those needs.\n\nFinal response structure:\n\n- No more tool calls now; this is the final user-facing response.\n\n- Direct, useful: Summarize creation, list what's there, suggest next steps.\n\n- Lead-in was already done pre-tool; now outcome based.\n\nIn the human message, antagonist is \"The Shadow King\", not \"Last\".\n\nIn my tool call, I had \"The Shadow King\".\n\nCounts include 1 document – probably auto-created context_document from description.\n\nResponse should:\n\n- Confirm creation with ID.\n\n- List entities accurately.\n\n- Offer next steps, like working on a task, since now in project context.\n\nKeep it proactive for project work.Great!\n\nI've created the project **\"The Last Ember\"** (ID: `b4dc45fe-9604-490b-8424-05714cbbd3d4`), a `project.creative.novel` in **planning** state.\n\nIt includes:\n\n- **1 goal**: \"Write and complete the fantasy novel 'The Last Ember'\"\n\n- **7 tasks** under that goal:\n\n- Create magic system based on metal and fire\n\n- Map out the kingdom of Aethermoor\n\n- Outline first three chapters\n\n- Research medieval blacksmithing techniques\n\n- Design the prophecy that drives the plot\n\n- **1 document** (project context summary)\n\n- **9 edges** (connections)\n\nWe're now focused here in the project."
		},
		{
			"role": "user",
			"content": "Finished chapter 2 today - 4,500 words. The scene where Elena discovers the dragon forge went really well.\n\nIssues to address:\n\n- Need to strengthen the dialogue between Elena and Master Thorne\n- The pacing in the middle feels slow\n- Add more sensory details about the forge\n\nChapter 3 plans:\n\n- Elena's first attempt at magical forging\n- Introduce the Shadow King's herald\n- Foreshadow the prophecy\n\nAlso need to go back and fix continuity issue - Elena's age mentioned as 16 in chapter 1 but 17 in chapter 2."
		}

			"type": "function",
			"function": {
				"name": "change_chat_context",
				"parameters": {
					"type": "object",
					"required": ["target"],
					"properties": {
						"reason": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Brief user-visible reason for the context change, grounded in the latest request."
						},
						"target": {
							"enum": ["global", "project"],
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Target context. Use global for workspace/all-projects zoom-out. Use project for durable focus on one project."
						},
						"project_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Exact project UUID when zooming into a known project."
						},
						"project_query": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Project name or phrase to resolve when zooming into a project and project_id is unknown."
						}

				},
				"description": "Change the durable chat context when the latest user request should strategically zoom into one project or zoom out to the workspace.\n\nUse this early in the turn before answering when:\n- The user explicitly asks to zoom, switch, focus, go to a project, or go back to all projects.\n- The current context is global/workspace and the latest request is primarily about one identifiable project, so project tools should be loaded for the rest of the turn.\n- The current context is one project and the latest request is clearly about a different project, and the user appears to be moving focus rather than doing a brief comparison.\n- The user asks about all projects, workspace status, cross-project priorities, or wants to zoom out.\n\nDo not use this for ambiguous project names, one-off comparisons across multiple projects, or brief mentions that can be answered from current context. For project zoom-in, pass project_id if known or project_query if the name needs resolution. Ambiguous or missing project matches return candidates and do not change context."
			}

										"type": "string"
									},
									"rel": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"kind": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"intent": {
										"enum": ["containment", "semantic"],
										"type": "string"
									}
								}
							},
							"description": "Optional semantic or containment links"
						},
						"description": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Task description"
						},
						"assignee_handles": {
							"type": "array",
							"items": {
								"type": "string"
							},
							"description": "Active project member handles, e.g. @dj."
						},
						"assignee_actor_ids": {
							"type": "array",
							"items": {
								"type": "string"
							},
							"description": "Active project member actor UUIDs. Max 10."
						},
						"supporting_milestone_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Optional milestone UUID"
						}
					},
					"additionalProperties": false
				},
				"description": "Create a new task in the ontology system.\nCreates a task within a project and optionally assigns it to a plan and collaborators.\nUse when the user explicitly asks to add/track/remind, or when the item is future human work outside this chat.\nDo not create tasks for research, brainstorming, summarizing, or drafting you can do now; do the work instead.\nAlways include project_id and a concrete title. Link to parent/plan/goal/milestone only when known. Use assignees only for known project members.\nLoad task_management for complex task flows."
			},
			"parameters": {
				"type": "object",
				"required": ["project_id", "title"],
				"properties": {
					"props": {
						"type": "object",
						"description": "Additional JSON properties"
					},
					"title": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "Task title"
					},
					"due_at": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "ISO due date"
					},
					"parent": {
						"type": "object",
						"properties": {
							"id": {
								"type": "string"
							},
							"kind": {
								"type": "string"
							},
							"is_primary": {
								"type": "boolean"
							}
						},
						"description": "Optional primary containment parent"
					},
					"goal_id": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "Optional goal UUID"
					},
					"parents": {
						"type": "array",
						"items": {
							"type": "object",
							"properties": {
								"id": {
									"type": "string"
								},
								"kind": {
									"type": "string"
								},
								"is_primary": {
									"type": "boolean"
								}
							}
						},
						"description": "Optional containment parents"
					},
					"plan_id": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "Optional plan UUID"
					},
					"priority": {
						"type": "number",
						"description": "Priority 1-5. Default 3."
					},
					"start_at": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "ISO start date"
					},
					"type_key": {
						"type": "string",
						"default": "task.execute",
						"description": "Task type key. Default task.execute."
					},
					"state_key": {
						"type": "string",
						"default": "todo",
						"description": "Initial state. Valid: todo, in_progress, blocked, done"
					},
					"project_id": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "Project UUID"
					},
					"connections": {
						"type": "array",
						"items": {
							"type": "object",
							"properties": {
								"id": {
									"type": "string"
								},
								"rel": {
									"type": "string"
								},
								"kind": {
									"type": "string"
								},
								"intent": {
									"enum": ["containment", "semantic"],
									"type": "string"
								}
							}
						},
						"description": "Optional semantic or containment links"
					},
					"description": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "Task description"
					},
					"assignee_handles": {
						"type": "array",
						"items": {
							"type": "string"
						},
						"description": "Active project member handles, e.g. @dj."
					},
					"assignee_actor_ids": {
						"type": "array",
						"items": {
							"type": "string"
						},
						"description": "Active project member actor UUIDs. Max 10."
					},
					"supporting_milestone_id": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "Optional milestone UUID"
					}
				},
				"additionalProperties": false
			},
			"description": "Create a new task in the ontology system.\nCreates a task within a project and optionally assigns it to a plan and collaborators.\nUse when the user explicitly asks to add/track/remind, or when the item is future human work outside this chat.\nDo not create tasks for research, brainstorming, summarizing, or drafting you can do now; do the work instead.\nAlways include project_id and a concrete title. Link to parent/plan/goal/milestone only when known. Use assignees only for known project members.\nLoad task_management for complex task flows."
		},
		{
			"name": "update_onto_task",
			"type": "function",
			"function": {
				"name": "update_onto_task",
				"parameters": {
					"type": "object",
					"required": ["task_id"],
					"properties": {
						"props": {
							"type": "object",
							"description": "Properties to merge with existing props"
						},
						"title": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "New task title"
						},
						"due_at": {
							"type": ["string", "null"],
							"description": "New due date (ISO format) or null to clear"
						},
						"goal_id": {
							"type": ["string", "null"],
							"description": "Optional goal UUID that this task supports (null clears)"
						},
						"task_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Task UUID (required)"
						},
						"priority": {
							"type": "number",
							"description": "New priority (1-5)"
						},
						"start_at": {
							"type": ["string", "null"],
							"description": "New start date (ISO format) or null to clear"
						},
						"type_key": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Work mode taxonomy: task.{work_mode}[.{specialization}]. Modes: execute, create, refine, research, review, coordinate, admin, plan."
						},
						"state_key": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "New state (todo, in_progress, blocked, done)"
						},
						"project_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Optional project UUID used for assignee handle resolution. If omitted, the task project is resolved automatically."
						},
						"description": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "New description"
						},
						"assignee_handles": {
							"type": "array",
							"items": {
								"type": "string"
							},
							"description": "Optional assignee handles like [\"@jim\"]. Resolved against active project members by name/email local-part."
						},
						"assignee_actor_ids": {
							"type": "array",
							"items": {
								"type": "string"
							},
							"description": "Optional full replacement assignee actor ID list (UUIDs) for active project members only. Prefer assignee_handles unless IDs were just retrieved from project members. Use [] to clear assignees. Max 10."
						},
						"supporting_milestone_id": {
							"type": ["string", "null"],
							"description": "Optional milestone UUID this task targets (null clears)"
						}
					}
				},
				"description": "Update an existing task in the ontology system.\nCan modify title, description, state, priority, assignees, scheduling, and custom properties.\nOnly updates fields that are provided - omitted fields remain unchanged."
			},
			"parameters": {
				"type": "object",
				"required": ["task_id"],
				"properties": {
					"props": {
						"type": "object",
						"description": "Properties to merge with existing props"
					},
					"title": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "New task title"
					},
					"due_at": {
						"type": ["string", "null"],
						"description": "New due date (ISO format) or null to clear"
					},
					"goal_id": {
						"type": ["string", "null"],
						"description": "Optional goal UUID that this task supports (null clears)"
					},
					"task_id": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "Task UUID (required)"
					},
					"priority": {
						"type": "number",
						"description": "New priority (1-5)"
					},
					"start_at": {
						"type": ["string", "null"],
						"description": "New start date (ISO format) or null to clear"
					},
					"type_key": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "Work mode taxonomy: task.{work_mode}[.{specialization}]. Modes: execute, create, refine, research, review, coordinate, admin, plan."
					},
					"state_key": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "New state (todo, in_progress, blocked, done)"
					},
					"project_id": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "Optional project UUID used for assignee handle resolution. If omitted, the task project is resolved automatically."
					},
					"description": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "New description"
					},
					"assignee_handles": {
						"type": "array",
						"items": {
							"type": "string"
						},
						"description": "Optional assignee handles like [\"@jim\"]. Resolved against active project members by name/email local-part."
					},
					"assignee_actor_ids": {
						"type": "array",
						"items": {
							"type": "string"
						},
						"description": "Optional full replacement assignee actor ID list (UUIDs) for active project members only. Prefer assignee_handles unless IDs were just retrieved from project members. Use [] to clear assignees. Max 10."
					},
					"supporting_milestone_id": {
						"type": ["string", "null"],
						"description": "Optional milestone UUID this task targets (null clears)"
					}
				}
			},
			"description": "Update an existing task in the ontology system.\nCan modify title, description, state, priority, assignees, scheduling, and custom properties.\nOnly updates fields that are provided - omitted fields remain unchanged."
		},

		{
			"name": "create_onto_document",
			"type": "function",
			"function": {
				"name": "create_onto_document",
				"parameters": {
					"type": "object",
					"required": ["project_id", "title", "description"],
					"properties": {
						"props": {
							"type": "object",
							"description": "Additional properties/metadata"
						},
						"title": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Document title (required)"
						},
						"parent": {
							"type": "object",
							"properties": {
								"id": {
									"type": "string"
								},
								"kind": {
									"type": "string"
								},
								"is_primary": {
									"type": "boolean"
								}
							},
							"description": "Optional parent reference for semantic linking (legacy)"
						},
						"content": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Markdown body content stored in the content column"
						},
						"parents": {
							"type": "array",
							"items": {
								"type": "object",
								"properties": {
									"id": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"kind": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"is_primary": {
										"type": "boolean"
									}
								}
							},
							"description": "Optional multiple semantic parents (legacy)"
						},
						"position": {
							"type": "number",
							"description": "Position among siblings (0-indexed). Omit to place at end."
						},
						"type_key": {
							"type": "string",
							"default": "document.default",
							"description": "Document type taxonomy (required): document.{family}[.{variant}]\nFamilies: context (project/brief), knowledge (research/brain_dump), notes (meeting_notes/rfc), spec (product/technical), reference (handbook/sop), intake (client/project).\nExamples: document.context.project, document.knowledge.research, document.spec.technical"
						},
						"parent_id": {
							"type": ["string", "null"],
							"description": "Parent document ID to place this document under in the tree hierarchy. Null or omitted places at root level."
						},
						"state_key": {
							"type": "string",
							"default": "draft",
							"description": "Document state (draft, in_review, ready, published, archived)"
						},
						"project_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Project UUID (required)"
						},
						"description": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Short summary of the document (required)"
						}
					}
				},
				"description": "Create a new document in the ontology system.\nUse for briefs, specs, context docs, or research artifacts linked to a project.\nDocuments are organized in a hierarchical tree structure. Use parent_id to place the document under a parent folder."
			},
			"parameters": {
				"type": "object",
				"required": ["project_id", "title", "description"],
				"properties": {
					"props": {
						"type": "object",
						"description": "Additional properties/metadata"
					},
					"title": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "Document title (required)"
					},
					"parent": {
						"type": "object",
						"properties": {
							"id": {
								"type": "string"
							},
							"kind": {
								"type": "string"
							},
							"is_primary": {
								"type": "boolean"
							}
						},
						"description": "Optional parent reference for semantic linking (legacy)"
					},
					"content": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "Markdown body content stored in the content column"
					},
					"parents": {
						"type": "array",
						"items": {
							"type": "object",
							"properties": {
								"id": {
									"type": "string"
								},
								"kind": {
									"type": "string"
								},
								"is_primary": {
									"type": "boolean"
								}
							}
						},
						"description": "Optional multiple semantic parents (legacy)"
					},
					"position": {
						"type": "number",
						"description": "Position among siblings (0-indexed). Omit to place at end."
					},
					"type_key": {
						"type": "string",
						"default": "document.default",
						"description": "Document type taxonomy (required): document.{family}[.{variant}]\nFamilies: context (project/brief), knowledge (research/brain_dump), notes (meeting_notes/rfc), spec (product/technical), reference (handbook/sop), intake (client/project).\nExamples: document.context.project, document.knowledge.research, document.spec.technical"
					},
					"parent_id": {
						"type": ["string", "null"],
						"description": "Parent document ID to place this document under in the tree hierarchy. Null or omitted places at root level."
					},
					"state_key": {
						"type": "string",
						"default": "draft",
						"description": "Document state (draft, in_review, ready, published, archived)"
					},
					"project_id": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "Project UUID (required)"
					},
					"description": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "Short summary of the document (required)"
					}
				}
			},
			"description": "Create a new document in the ontology system.\nUse for briefs, specs, context docs, or research artifacts linked to a project.\nDocuments are organized in a hierarchical tree structure. Use parent_id to place the document under a parent folder."
		},
		{
			"name": "update_onto_document",
			"type": "function",
			"function": {
				"name": "update_onto_document",
				"parameters": {
					"type": "object",
					"required": ["document_id"],
					"properties": {
						"props": {
							"type": "object",
							"description": "Metadata fields to merge into document props"
						},
						"title": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "New document title"
						},
						"content": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Markdown content to store in the content column. Required when update_strategy is append or merge_llm."
						},
						"type_key": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "New document type key"
						},
						"state_key": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Document state (draft, in_review, ready, published, archived)"
						},
						"description": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Short summary of the document"
						},
						"document_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Document UUID (required)"
						},
						"update_strategy": {
							"enum": ["replace", "append", "merge_llm"],
							"type": "string",
							"default": "replace",
							"description": "How to apply content: 'replace' (default), 'append', or 'merge_llm' to intelligently merge with existing content."
						},
						"merge_instructions": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Optional guidance when merging content (e.g., keep headers, preserve tables, integrate research notes). Used with append/merge_llm, but does not replace the required content."
						}
					},
					"additionalProperties": false
				},
				"description": "Update an existing ontology document. Use for edits to titles, states, body markdown, or metadata. For update_strategy \"append\" or \"merge_llm\", include non-empty content/body_markdown/markdown/body/text. merge_instructions alone is not content. Append example: update_onto_document({ document_id, content: \"## Progress Updates\\n\\n- Chapter 2 complete...\", update_strategy: \"append\", merge_instructions: \"Append under Progress Updates; preserve existing sections.\" })"
			},
			"parameters": {
				"type": "object",
				"required": ["document_id"],
				"properties": {
					"props": {
						"type": "object",
						"description": "Metadata fields to merge into document props"
					},
					"title": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "New document title"
					},
					"content": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "Markdown content to store in the content column. Required when update_strategy is append or merge_llm."
					},
					"type_key": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "New document type key"
					},
					"state_key": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "Document state (draft, in_review, ready, published, archived)"
					},
					"description": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "Short summary of the document"
					},
					"document_id": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "Document UUID (required)"
					},
					"update_strategy": {
						"enum": ["replace", "append", "merge_llm"],
						"type": "string",
						"default": "replace",
						"description": "How to apply content: 'replace' (default), 'append', or 'merge_llm' to intelligently merge with existing content."
					},
					"merge_instructions": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "Optional guidance when merging content (e.g., keep headers, preserve tables, integrate research notes). Used with append/merge_llm, but does not replace the required content."
					}
				},
				"additionalProperties": false
			},
			"description": "Update an existing ontology document. Use for edits to titles, states, body markdown, or metadata. For update_strategy \"append\" or \"merge_llm\", include non-empty content/body_markdown/markdown/body/text. merge_instructions alone is not content. Append example: update_onto_document({ document_id, content: \"## Progress Updates\\n\\n- Chapter 2 complete...\", update_strategy: \"append\", merge_instructions: \"Append under Progress Updates; preserve existing sections.\" })"
		},
		{
			"name": "get_document_tree",
			"type": "function",
			"function": {
				"name": "get_document_tree",
				"parameters": {
					"type": "object",
					"required": ["project_id"],
					"properties": {
						"project_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Project ID to get the document tree for (required)"
						},
						"include_content": {
							"type": "boolean",
							"default": false,
							"description": "Include full document content bodies. Only applies when include_documents is true."
						},
						"include_documents": {
							"type": "boolean",
							"default": false,
							"description": "Include document metadata and unlinked document list. Set true when you need titles or orphaned docs."
						}
					}
				},
				"description": "Get the hierarchical document tree structure for a project.\nReturns the tree structure; set include_documents to include document metadata and unlinked docs.\nDocuments are organized in a wiki-like tree structure with folders (documents that have children) and leaf documents."
			},
			"parameters": {
				"type": "object",
				"required": ["project_id"],
				"properties": {
					"project_id": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "Project ID to get the document tree for (required)"
					},
					"include_content": {
						"type": "boolean",
						"default": false,
						"description": "Include full document content bodies. Only applies when include_documents is true."
					},
					"include_documents": {
						"type": "boolean",
						"default": false,
						"description": "Include document metadata and unlinked document list. Set true when you need titles or orphaned docs."
					}
				}
			},
			"description": "Get the hierarchical document tree structure for a project.\nReturns the tree structure; set include_documents to include document metadata and unlinked docs.\nDocuments are organized in a wiki-like tree structure with folders (documents that have children) and leaf documents."
		},
		{
			"type": "function",
			"function": {
				"name": "move_document_in_tree",
				"parameters": {
					"type": "object",
					"required": ["project_id", "document_id"],
					"properties": {
						"project_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Project UUID (required)"
						},
						"document_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Document UUID to move (required)"
						},
						"new_position": {
							"type": "number",
							"default": 0,
							"description": "Position among siblings (0-indexed). Omit to place at top."
						},
						"new_parent_id": {
							"type": ["string", "null"],
							"description": "New parent document ID (null or omitted places the document at root level)."
						}
					}
				},
				"description": "Move or insert an existing document within the project's doc_structure.\nUse this to nest existing or unlinked documents under a parent or reorder siblings.\nIf new_parent_id is null or omitted, the document is placed at root level.\nAlways pass the exact document_id from get_document_tree/list_onto_documents (do not pass document titles)."
			}
		}
	],

		"entity_id": "b4dc45fe-9604-490b-8424-05714cbbd3d4",

		"context_type": "project",
		"project_focus": {
			"focusType": "project-wide",
			"projectId": "b4dc45fe-9604-490b-8424-05714cbbd3d4",
````
